entry.title: Game Undefeatability Document (GUD)
entry.summary: Ask yourself in your heart of hearts: “WHY is there a near-ZERO chance that MY GAME will FLOP?”
entry.content:
Ask yourself in your heart of hearts: “WHY is there a near-ZERO chance that MY GAME will FLOP?”

And if the answer reveals a non-zero chance… well, you need to get GUD.

tip

Victorious warriors win first and then go to war, while defeated warriors go to war first and then seek to win.

Sun Tzu

The content that follows is a living example of the philosophy we preach in the studio. This document will undergo incremental improvements throughout the game development cycle.

Project: Agony Dolls (WIP)
Version: 2026.05.15.A.A.A

Why does the game hook instantly?​

(First 5–15 minutes experience)

Why are the characters compelling?​

(Design, personality, progression fantasy, emotional connection)

Why does navigation feel fluid?​

After fully completing PotionCraft, I fell so in love with their limited set of locations and the store navigation that I decided to just take this approach and adapt it for my game about a voodoo doll shop.

In short, the world of PotionCraft consists of 2D locations of different rooms, with the camera looking straight down at a 90-degree angle. To move between rooms, the player uses the WASD keys and essentially "flies" from the bedroom to the laboratory, then to the basement or behind the sales counter. There’s no default walking character like in most game engine templates — only the camera.

And these are exactly the kind of design limitations that bring ergonomics to the game, significantly improving the player’s quality of life. The familiar movement keybinds from larger AAA games work predictably, yet are much simpler at the same time.

Why is core gameplay addictive to master?​

(Combat, building, puzzle, racing — whatever the main activity is)

Why is progression challenging and rewarding?​

(Power curve, sense of growth, meaningful choices, dopamine balance)

Why is the world alive and worth exploring?​

(Exploration, discovery, atmosphere, secrets, reactivity)

Why will players return for dozens of sessions?​

(Long-term retention hooks, daily/weekly rewards, mastery ceiling, community)

Why does the game feel fair?​

(Difficulty curve, feedback, failure recovery, anti-frustration systems)

Why is the experience memorable and shareable?​

(Moments of awe, stories players will tell, social virality, uniqueness)

entry.title: Am I done with the games?
entry.summary: My BIG problem is that gamedev means way more to me than just a hobby, but no matter what angle I approach it from, I’m extremely bad at making games — YET I CANNOT simply give up and quit.
entry.content:
My BIG problem is that gamedev means way more to me than just a hobby, but no matter what angle I approach it from, I’m extremely bad at making games — YET I CANNOT simply give up and quit.

A short list of my failures:

  • 2 failed playtests on Steam
  • 2 failed demos on Steam (hey, I actually got further than playtests!)
  • 5 unfinished game jams on itch.io
  • ~25 dead prototypes in UE5
  • ~5 dead prototypes in Godot

Like many others, I DREAMED of making a simple, fun little game, releasing it, and earning at least some money. But even that hope died about six months ago.

DUMB

And yet here I am, literally standing on completely scorched earth, somehow still believing I can create an oasis.

Since it’s already obvious that I’ll die before I ever quit this, I’ve decided to turn my entire bumpy, crater-filled journey into an online monument. Whether it ends up being a tragicomedy that warns beginners away, or a story with a happy ending — that depends only on what happens next.

For now, this post is just me admitting to myself: the only jam I will never skip is my own lifelong jam.
